entries.meaning: The science of posing and answering data questions related to business.
entries.scenario: A company wants to know not just what its sales numbers say, but why they matter and what to do next - turning that question into a rigorous data investigation. What field is this?

entries.meaning: The type of analytics that summarizes and reports historical data - answering 'what happened?'
entries.scenario: A retailer combines sales and loyalty data just to see which products each customer segment already prefers. What type of analytics is this?

entries.meaning: The type of analytics that uses historical patterns to forecast future outcomes - answering 'what will happen?'
entries.scenario: A firm uses past sales patterns to forecast demand for next quarter. What type of analytics is this?

entries.meaning: The type of analytics that recommends the best action given goals and constraints - answering 'what should we do?'
entries.scenario: An airline doesn't just predict demand - it uses optimization to decide exactly which dynamic price to set for every seat. What type of analytics is this?

entries.meaning: Reasoning from samples to conclusions under uncertainty - one of the four core tool areas analysts draw on.
entries.scenario: An analyst infers what an entire customer base thinks from a survey of only 500 people. What broad toolset makes that leap valid?

entries.meaning: Collecting data from a representative subset of a population, allowing inference with some sampling error.
entries.scenario: Instead of contacting every single customer, a company polls a representative sample of 1,000 and infers what the rest likely think. What method is this?

entries.meaning: Collecting data from every single unit in a population - accurate, but costly and time-consuming.
entries.scenario: A government counts every single citizen in the country rather than a sample of them. What is this complete count called?

entries.meaning: Data that is organized into clear fields, like transaction records or spreadsheets.
entries.scenario: A sales log with a clean row for every transaction and a column for each field is easy for software to query directly. What kind of data format is this?

entries.meaning: To extract data from sources like databases, APIs, or websites - a key step in the data collection process.
entries.scenario: An analyst writes a script to scrape data from an online platform and pull records from an API. What is this technical step called?

entries.meaning: One of the four Vs of big data - the sheer quantity of data generated.
entries.scenario: A social network generates billions of posts every single day - far too much for any one machine to handle. Which of the four Vs of big data does this describe?

entries.meaning: One of the four Vs of big data - the speed at which data arrives.
entries.scenario: A stock exchange's trading data streams in thousands of updates per second, faster than any single system could process one at a time. Which of the four Vs is this?

entries.meaning: One of the four Vs of big data - the mix of formats data comes in, like text, images, and logs.
entries.scenario: A company's data includes customer reviews, product photos, call-center audio, and web logs, all in totally different formats. Which of the four Vs of big data is this?

entries.meaning: One of the four Vs of big data - the trustworthiness and noise level of the data.
entries.scenario: Some sensor readings in a dataset are clearly broken or contradictory, so an analyst has to judge how much to trust them before using them. Which of the four Vs of big data is this?

entries.meaning: An open-source framework for storing and processing big data across clusters of computers.
entries.scenario: A company needs to store petabytes of log data across hundreds of cheap machines instead of one expensive server. Which big-data framework is built for exactly that?

entries.meaning: A big-data processing engine known for fast, in-memory computation across clusters.
entries.scenario: An analytics team can't afford to wait for slow disk-based processing every time they rerun a big-data job, so they switch to an in-memory cluster engine. Which tool is this?

entries.meaning: A data collection method that gathers data from every single unit in a population, such as a census.
entries.scenario: A national census contacts every household in the country rather than a sample of them. What is this complete-coverage method called?

entries.meaning: A data collection method where data is generated under controlled conditions to test a hypothesis.
entries.scenario: Researchers test five different fertilizers across different soil types under controlled conditions to see which works best. What data collection method is this?

entries.meaning: Data with no predefined format, like free text, images, audio, or video - harder to analyze directly.
entries.scenario: A dataset of customer service call recordings and product photos can't be queried like a spreadsheet the way clean transaction records can. What kind of data format is this?

entries.meaning: Data where a single unit is tracked over multiple points in time.
entries.scenario: An analyst studies one company's monthly revenue over the past five years. What data dimension describes tracking one unit over time like this?

entries.meaning: Data where many different units are each measured at a single point in time.
entries.scenario: An analyst compares 500 different companies' revenue for this quarter only, with no history attached. What data dimension describes measuring many units at one moment like this?

entries.meaning: Data where multiple units are each tracked over multiple points in time.
entries.scenario: An analyst tracks 500 companies' revenue every quarter for five years straight. What is this richer combination of cross-sectional and time-series data called?

entries.meaning: A data point or value that looks implausible or wildly different from the rest, worth checking before trusting the data.
entries.scenario: While validating a dataset, an analyst spots a customer record listing an age of -12. What should that impossible value immediately be flagged as?

entries.meaning: A reliability check confirming that the same measurement, repeated, gives the same result.
entries.scenario: To make sure a satisfaction survey is reliable, a researcher gives the same customer the same questionnaire twice a week apart and checks the answers match. What kind of check is this?

entries.meaning: A model that assigns credit for a conversion across multiple ads or channels, such as first-click or last-click.
entries.scenario: A customer sees a display ad, then a social ad, then clicks a search ad and buys - and marketing needs to decide which ad gets credit for the sale. What kind of model solves that?

entries.meaning: A programming model for processing huge datasets in parallel across a cluster of machines.
entries.scenario: A big-data job splits a massive log file across hundreds of machines, processes each piece in parallel, then combines the results. What is this processing model called?

entries.meaning: A machine learning approach that learns from labeled examples to predict an outcome.
entries.scenario: A model is trained on thousands of past loan applications explicitly labeled 'defaulted' or 'repaid' so it can predict new applicants. What kind of learning is this?

entries.meaning: A machine learning approach that finds structure - like groups or patterns - in data with no labels at all.
entries.scenario: An algorithm is given raw customer purchase data with no labels and asked to find natural groupings on its own. What kind of learning is this?

entries.meaning: A data scale that categorizes data with no inherent order, such as colors or categories.
entries.scenario: A dataset labels each product's color as green, yellow, or red - there's no sense in which one color 'ranks higher' than another. What scale is this?

entries.meaning: A data scale with a meaningful order, but where the gaps between categories aren't uniformly measurable.
entries.scenario: A survey ranks customer education as Illiterate, Primary, or Graduate - clearly ordered, but the 'distance' between levels isn't a fixed, measurable amount. What scale is this?

entries.meaning: Drawing conclusions only from the survivors of a selection process, while the missing cases are excluded.
entries.scenario: Engineers plan to armor a WWII bomber's wings, where returning planes show the most bullet holes - but planes hit in the engines never made it back to be counted at all. What reasoning error nearly led them to armor the wrong part?

entries.meaning: An error where the sampling frame used to collect data misses part of the population it's meant to represent.
entries.scenario: A phone survey about internet habits accidentally excludes everyone who only owns a mobile phone with no landline. What kind of data collection error is that?

entries.meaning: To confirm a data finding by checking it against a second, independent source.
entries.scenario: Before trusting a survey result, a researcher also checks it against government statistics and a completely separate dataset. What validation practice is this?

entries.meaning: A machine learning approach that learns through trial, reward, and feedback over time, rather than labels.
entries.scenario: A system tries different actions, gets a reward signal for good outcomes and a penalty for bad ones, and gradually learns the best policy through repeated trial and error. What kind of learning is this?

entries.meaning: Data-driven decision making as a repeating cycle, not a single one-off report.
entries.scenario: After Wald's recommendation saved bomber crews, that same decision fed straight into the next question the military needed answered. What does this show analytics really is, rather than a single one-time report?

entries.meaning: Research done when the problem itself isn't even clear yet - so-called 'unknown-unknowns.'
entries.scenario: A country head only knows 'sales are down' but has no idea why - is it the product, the brand, or the price? Identifying the real problem is itself the problem here. What type of research fits this situation?

entries.meaning: Research that tests whether one specific action produces one specific effect.
entries.scenario: A manager doesn't just want to know who buys the product - she wants to know whether cutting the price would actually raise sales. What type of research directly tests that cause-and-effect question?

entries.meaning: A data quality problem where respondents misremember details or give socially desirable rather than accurate answers.
entries.scenario: Survey respondents asked about their spending three months ago tend to round numbers and remember themselves more favorably than the receipts would show. What kind of bias is that?

entries.meaning: A data quality problem where people contacted for data simply don't respond, skewing the sample toward those who do.
entries.scenario: Only the most satisfied (or most furious) customers bother to fill out the feedback survey, while everyone in between stays silent. What kind of collection problem does this create?

entries.meaning: A data scale with equal, measurable gaps between values, but no true zero - differences are meaningful, ratios aren't.
entries.scenario: 20 degrees isn't 'twice as hot' as 10 degrees, even though the numbers say so - because zero doesn't mean 'no temperature at all.' What data scale is temperature an example of?

entries.meaning: A data scale with equal intervals AND a true zero point, so both differences and ratios are meaningful.
entries.scenario: A runner who finishes in 10 seconds really is twice as fast as one who finishes in 20 seconds, because 0 seconds genuinely means 'no time elapsed.' What data scale is race time an example of?
